label: fiction
title: Last Call
charge: Assault causing injury after a fight outside a bar

beats.text: The complainant, his jaw wired shut, testifies that the accused set on him without warning, 'out of nowhere.'
beats.reveal_note: A gravely injured man is compelling, but memory of a fast, drunken, painful moment is unreliable — and here it is flatly contradicted by the footage.

beats.text: The bar's outside camera shows the complainant swinging a bottle at the accused's head first; the accused's single punch lands only after.
beats.reveal_note: The camera does not take a side. It shows unlawful force came first, and one blow answered it.

beats.text: A friend of the complainant says the accused 'looked like he was after a fight' and is known to have a temper.
beats.reveal_note: A partisan witness describing a mood. A temper is not this act, and 'looked like' is not evidence of who struck first.

beats.text: A bartender with no connection to either man confirms the bottle came first and that the accused stepped back before throwing the single punch.
beats.reveal_note: A neutral account, matching the tape: the accused retreated, then defended himself.

beats.text: The judge instructs that a person met with unlawful force may defend himself, so long as the force he uses is proportionate.
beats.reveal_note: The frame for everything else: one punch to answer a swung bottle is not obviously excessive.

twist: A broken jaw and a furious victim make the accused look like the aggressor. But the footage doesn't take sides: the bottle came first, and the punch that followed was a man protecting his head. A temper and a hard face are not evidence of who started it.
